title: Definitions
description: GDPR defines key terms: personal data, processing, controller, processor, data subject, consent, and special categories of data.
purpose: Establishes the foundational terminology for interpreting all GDPR obligations.
implementationGuidance: Familiarise all privacy programme staff with GDPR definitions. Map internal terminology to GDPR definitions. Ensure contracts with processors use GDPR-aligned definitions.
bestPractices: Align internal data governance terminology with GDPR definitions
Include definitions in privacy training for all staff

title: Principles Relating to Processing of Personal Data
description: Personal data shall be processed lawfully, fairly, transparently, for specified purposes, minimised, accurately maintained, time-limited, and with integrity and confidentiality (accountability principle).
purpose: Establishes the seven core principles that govern all personal data processing under GDPR.
implementationGuidance: Apply all seven principles to every processing activity: lawfulness, fairness, transparency; purpose limitation; data minimisation; accuracy; storage limitation; integrity and confidentiality; accountability. Document compliance with each principle in your RoPA.
evidenceExamples: Completed RoPA entries for each processing activity
bestPractices: Use the 7 principles as a checklist for every new processing activity
Review RoPA annually to verify continued compliance with each principle
Make the principles part of privacy by design assessments

title: Lawfulness of Processing
description: Processing shall be lawful only if based on one of six legal bases: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests.
purpose: Ensures every processing activity has a documented legal justification.
implementationGuidance: Identify and document the lawful basis for every processing activity in the RoPA. Legitimate interests require a Legitimate Interests Assessment (LIA). Consent must meet the requirements of Article 7.
evidenceExamples: RoPA showing lawful basis for each activity
LIA documents for legitimate interests processing
bestPractices: Document lawful basis during system design — not after the fact
Conduct LIAs for all legitimate interests processing activities
Never default to consent when another basis is more appropriate

title: Conditions for Consent
description: Consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. It must be as easy to withdraw as to give. Pre-ticked boxes and bundled consent are prohibited.
purpose: Ensures consent used as a lawful basis genuinely reflects individuals' free choices.
implementationGuidance: Implement consent mechanisms that are clearly optional, granular (separate for each purpose), presented before data collection, easily withdrawable, and logged with timestamp and scope.
evidenceExamples: Consent capture UI/UX showing pre-ticked boxes are absent
Consent records with timestamp, scope, and mechanism
bestPractices: Never bundle consent for unrelated purposes
Store granular consent records with full audit trail
Test withdrawal mechanism with users to verify ease of use

title: Conditions Applicable to Child's Consent in Relation to Information Society Services
description: For children under 16 (or lower age set by Member States, minimum 13), consent must be given by or authorised by the holder of parental responsibility.
purpose: Provides enhanced protection for children's personal data in online services.
implementationGuidance: Determine the applicable age threshold in all jurisdictions you operate in. Implement age verification and parental consent mechanisms. Design services with children's privacy and safety in mind.
bestPractices: Apply the most restrictive age threshold (13) as a default
Use age-appropriate design principles for all services accessible to children
Document the jurisdiction-specific age thresholds applicable to your services

title: Processing of Special Categories of Personal Data
description: Processing of special categories (health, biometric, racial/ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, sexual orientation, etc.) is prohibited unless explicit consent or another specific exemption applies.
purpose: Provides heightened protection for the most sensitive categories of personal data.
implementationGuidance: Identify all special category data processing. Justify each under an Article 9(2) exemption. Document justification in the RoPA. Apply enhanced security controls to special category data. Conduct DPIAs for special category processing.
requiredDocuments: Enhanced security controls for special categories
evidenceExamples: RoPA entries for special category data with Article 9(2) basis
DPIA records for special category processing
Enhanced access controls for health/biometric data
bestPractices: Minimise special category data collection to the absolute minimum
Apply additional technical and organisational measures to special category data
Conduct DPIA for all special category processing activities

title: Processing of Criminal Convictions and Offences Data
description: Processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences or related security measures shall be carried out only under the control of official authority or when authorised by Union or Member State law.
purpose: Restricts processing of highly sensitive criminal records data.
implementationGuidance: Assess whether your organisation processes any criminal convictions or offences data. If so, identify the specific legal authorisation. Implement enhanced controls for such data.
requiredDocuments: Legal authorisation for criminal data processing
evidenceExamples: Legal basis documentation for criminal data processing
Enhanced access controls for criminal records data
bestPractices: Avoid processing criminal data unless legally required
Document legal authorisation explicitly in the RoPA
Apply strictest access controls to any criminal records data

title: Transparent Information, Communication and Modalities for the Exercise of the Rights of the Data Subject
description: Information and communications relating to data subjects' rights must be concise, transparent, intelligible, and in plain language. Requests must be responded to without undue delay, and generally within one month.
purpose: Ensures data subjects can meaningfully understand and exercise their rights.
implementationGuidance: Develop clear, layered privacy notices. Implement procedures to respond to data subject requests within one month. Establish identity verification procedures for requests. Provide responses free of charge.
requiredDocuments: Privacy notice review for clarity and transparency
evidenceExamples: Readability test results for privacy notices
DSAR response records within one-month deadline
bestPractices: Use a Flesch-Kincaid readability test to verify notice clarity
Implement a dedicated portal for data subject requests
Track response times against the one-month deadline as a KPI

title: Information to Be Provided Where Personal Data Are Collected from the Data Subject
description: At the time of collection, the controller must provide the data subject with a defined set of information including identity of controller, purposes, legal basis, recipients, and rights.
purpose: Ensures individuals are fully informed at the point of data collection.
implementationGuidance: Ensure privacy notices at point of collection include all Article 13 required elements: controller identity, DPO contact, purposes, legal basis, recipients, retention periods, and all applicable rights.
evidenceExamples: Privacy notice reviewed against Article 13 checklist
Evidence of notice displayed at data collection points
DPO contact published in privacy notice
bestPractices: Perform a legal review of privacy notices against the Article 13 checklist
Update notices immediately when any Article 13 element changes
Provide just-in-time notice at each specific data collection point

title: Information to Be Provided Where Personal Data Have Not Been Obtained from the Data Subject
description: Where personal data have not been obtained directly from the data subject, the controller must provide the Article 13 information within one month of obtaining the data.
purpose: Ensures individuals are informed even when their data is obtained from third-party sources.
implementationGuidance: Identify all processing activities where data is obtained from sources other than the individual. Implement procedures to provide Article 14 information within one month. Document compliance.
requiredDocuments: Notification evidence for indirect data collection
evidenceExamples: Article 14 notices sent to data subjects
Notification timeline records showing compliance with one-month deadline
Third-party data source register with notification procedures
bestPractices: Map all indirect data sources and implement notification workflows
Use batch notification processes for large datasets obtained from third parties
Document when exceptions to notification apply (e.g., disproportionate effort)

title: Right of Access by the Data Subject
description: Data subjects have the right to obtain confirmation of whether personal data is being processed and, if so, access to the data and information about the processing.
purpose: Enables individuals to understand and verify what personal data is held about them.
implementationGuidance: Implement a DSAR (Data Subject Access Request) process. Verify requestor identity. Provide a copy of personal data and all Article 15 information within one month. Do not charge for initial requests.
evidenceExamples: DSAR records with response within one-month deadline
DSAR response examples showing all required information
bestPractices: Implement a self-service DSAR portal where feasible
Maintain a DSAR register tracking all requests and response dates
Test DSAR responses for completeness periodically

title: Right to Rectification
description: Data subjects have the right to obtain rectification of inaccurate personal data and to have incomplete data completed.
purpose: Ensures individuals can correct inaccurate or incomplete personal data held about them.
implementationGuidance: Implement a process to receive and fulfil rectification requests within one month. Verify corrections are accurate. Propagate corrections to processors and recipients where data was shared.
bestPractices: Implement data correction workflows in all systems holding personal data
Notify processors of corrections within a defined timeframe
Log all rectifications with before/after state for audit trail

title: Right to Erasure ('Right to be Forgotten')
description: Data subjects have the right to obtain erasure of personal data where grounds apply (consent withdrawn, data no longer necessary, etc.).
purpose: Enables individuals to have their personal data deleted when it is no longer needed or they withdraw consent.
implementationGuidance: Implement erasure request procedures. Assess whether grounds for erasure apply. Implement technical erasure capabilities including propagation to processors and backups. Document where erasure cannot be fully achieved.
evidenceExamples: Records where erasure was declined with documented grounds
bestPractices: Build erasure capabilities into systems from the start
Test erasure completeness across all data stores including backups
Log all erasure decisions (granted or declined) with reasoning

title: Right to Restriction of Processing
description: Data subjects have the right to restrict processing in certain circumstances (disputed accuracy, unlawful processing, data needed for legal claims).
purpose: Provides individuals with a means to pause data processing while other issues are resolved.
implementationGuidance: Implement a restriction flag or mechanism in data systems. Process only restricted data for the permitted purposes (storage, legal claims, exercising rights, protecting others). Notify data subjects before lifting restriction.
evidenceExamples: Records of permitted processing during restriction
bestPractices: Implement data restriction as a first-class feature in data management systems
Notify data subjects when restriction is lifted
Track all restriction requests and their resolution

title: Notification Obligation Regarding Rectification, Erasure or Restriction
description: The controller must communicate any rectification, erasure, or restriction to each recipient to whom the data was disclosed, unless impossible or involving disproportionate effort.
purpose: Ensures changes to personal data are propagated to all parties who received the data.
implementationGuidance: Maintain a record of all data disclosures to enable notification of recipients. Implement a process to notify recipients of rectification, erasure, or restriction. Document cases where notification is not feasible.
bestPractices: Maintain a current data disclosure register per data subject where feasible
Establish automated notification workflows for common correction scenarios
Document all cases where notification is impracticable with reasoning

title: Right to Data Portability
description: Data subjects have the right to receive their personal data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format, and to transmit it to another controller.
purpose: Enables individuals to move their personal data between service providers.
implementationGuidance: Implement data portability capabilities for data processed by automated means on the basis of consent or contract. Provide data in a structured format (JSON, CSV, XML). Do not charge for portability requests.
evidenceExamples: Portability request records with data provided
bestPractices: Build portability features into data management systems during development
Use standard formats (JSON, XML) for portability exports
Test portability exports for completeness and accuracy

title: Right to Object
description: Data subjects have the right to object to processing based on legitimate interests, or for direct marketing purposes.
purpose: Provides individuals with control over processing that may be conducted without their consent.
implementationGuidance: Implement opt-out mechanisms for direct marketing immediately upon request. For other legitimate interests processing, assess the objection and cease processing unless compelling legitimate grounds override. Document all objection decisions.
requiredDocuments: Objection assessment procedures for non-marketing processing
evidenceExamples: Direct marketing opt-out mechanism (unsubscribe links, etc.)
Records of processing cessation following valid objections
bestPractices: Implement one-click unsubscribe for all marketing communications
Process direct marketing objections immediately upon receipt
Document the assessment and outcome of non-marketing objections

title: Automated Individual Decision-Making, Including Profiling
description: Data subjects have the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produce legal or similarly significant effects.
purpose: Protects individuals from significant decisions made without human review.
implementationGuidance: Identify all automated decision-making processes with legal or similar significant effects. Obtain explicit consent or demonstrate another Article 22(2) exception applies. Implement human review mechanisms. Inform data subjects of logic involved.
requiredDocuments: Human review procedures for automated decisions
Explanation of logic for automated processing
evidenceExamples: Communications to data subjects about automated decisions
bestPractices: Implement human review as the default for consequential automated decisions
Document the logic and factors used in automated decisions
Conduct DPIAs for all automated decision-making systems

title: Responsibility of the Controller
description: Taking into account the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing, the controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure processing is performed in accordance with GDPR.
purpose: Establishes the controller's overall accountability for GDPR compliance.
implementationGuidance: Implement a privacy management programme covering policies, procedures, accountability measures, and staff training. Document all compliance measures. Demonstrate compliance to regulators when requested.
bestPractices: Implement a privacy by design framework
Conduct annual privacy programme assessments
Maintain an evidence library demonstrating compliance with each GDPR article

title: Data Protection by Design and by Default
description: Both at the time of design and at the time of processing, the controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures (such as data minimisation) to implement data protection principles effectively.
purpose: Ensures privacy is built into systems and processes from the outset.
implementationGuidance: Integrate privacy requirements into the SDLC. Conduct privacy impact assessments during system design. Default settings should be privacy-friendly. Apply data minimisation at the design stage.
evidenceExamples: Privacy by design assessment records for new systems
bestPractices: Conduct DPIAs during system design, not post-implementation
Make privacy-friendly defaults the standard — opt-in rather than opt-out
Include privacy requirements in acceptance testing criteria

title: Joint Controllers
description: Where two or more controllers jointly determine the purposes and means of processing, they shall be joint controllers and determine their respective responsibilities in a transparent arrangement.
purpose: Clarifies accountability when multiple parties share control of personal data processing.
implementationGuidance: Identify all joint controller arrangements. Execute written joint controller agreements defining each party's responsibilities. Make the essence of the arrangement available to data subjects.
requiredDocuments: Data subject notice of joint controller arrangement
evidenceExamples: Responsibility matrix for joint processing activities
Privacy notices disclosing joint controller relationships
bestPractices: Identify all potential joint controller arrangements during data mapping
Clearly define responsibilities for data subject rights fulfilment in joint agreements
Include joint controller arrangements in privacy notices

title: Representatives of Controllers or Processors not Established in the Union
description: Where Article 3(2) applies (processing of EU residents' data from outside EU), the controller or processor shall designate a representative in the Union.
purpose: Ensures EU residents can exercise their rights against non-EU organisations processing their data.
implementationGuidance: Assess whether your organisation requires an EU representative. If required, designate a representative in an EU Member State. Document the representative's mandate and publish contact details.
bestPractices: Assess applicability of Article 27 when entering EU markets
Designate an EU representative in a Member State aligned with your main processing activities
Ensure the representative is empowered to act on your behalf

title: Processor
description: Processing by a processor shall be governed by a contract (Data Processing Agreement) binding the processor to the controller with specific required terms including: processing only on controller's instructions, confidentiality, security, assistance, deletion/return, and audit rights.
purpose: Ensures processors are contractually bound to protect personal data on behalf of controllers.
implementationGuidance: Execute a GDPR-compliant DPA with every processor before sharing personal data. Include all mandatory Article 28(3) clauses. Review DPAs when processors or processing activities change.
bestPractices: Execute DPAs before any personal data is shared with processors
Maintain a central DPA repository with renewal tracking
Review all DPAs when GDPR requirements change (e.g., SCC updates)

title: Processing Under the Authority of the Controller or Processor
description: The processor and any person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data shall not process those data except on instructions from the controller.
purpose: Ensures processor employees only process personal data as authorised by the controller.
implementationGuidance: Implement procedures requiring processor employees to process data only on documented controller instructions. Prohibit processors from using personal data for their own purposes. Include in DPAs.
evidenceExamples: Records of processing instructions provided to processors
bestPractices: Provide clear, documented processing instructions to all processors
Include instructions in DPAs rather than relying on informal communications
Monitor processor compliance with instructions through audits

title: Records of Processing Activities
description: Each controller shall maintain a record of processing activities including name and contact details, purposes, data subjects, data categories, recipients, transfers, retention periods, and security measures.
purpose: Creates a comprehensive inventory of all personal data processing as the foundation for GDPR accountability.
implementationGuidance: Create and maintain a Record of Processing Activities (RoPA) covering all required Article 30 fields. Update the RoPA when processing activities change. Make available to supervisory authorities on request.
evidenceExamples: RoPA format covering all Article 30 fields
bestPractices: Maintain the RoPA in a purpose-built tool rather than a spreadsheet for scalability
Review and update the RoPA at least annually and whenever processing changes
Treat the RoPA as a living document rather than a one-time exercise

title: Cooperation with the Supervisory Authority
description: The controller, the processor and, where applicable, their representatives, shall cooperate, on request, with the supervisory authority.
purpose: Requires organisations to work constructively with data protection regulators.
implementationGuidance: Designate a point of contact for supervisory authority inquiries. Define processes for responding to authority requests. Maintain records and evidence library to support regulatory investigations.
evidenceExamples: Designated DPO or legal contact for regulatory inquiries
Evidence of cooperation in past regulatory interactions
bestPractices: Designate a DPO or legal contact for all supervisory authority communications
Respond to supervisory authority requests promptly and fully
Seek legal advice before responding to complex regulatory inquiries

title: Security of Processing
description: Taking into account state of the art and risks, controller and processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including pseudonymisation, encryption, resilience, and testing.
purpose: Establishes the security obligations for protecting personal data.
implementationGuidance: Conduct risk-based assessment of security measures. Implement: encryption at rest and in transit; pseudonymisation; access controls; resilience and redundancy; regular testing; incident response. Document all security measures.
requiredDocuments: Technical and organisational security measures (TOMs) documentation
evidenceExamples: Security testing results (penetration tests, vulnerability scans)
bestPractices: Document all TOMs in a structured format and review annually
Conduct penetration testing at least annually
Use encryption and pseudonymisation as default security measures for personal data

title: Notification of a Personal Data Breach to the Supervisory Authority
description: In the case of a personal data breach, the controller shall notify the competent supervisory authority without undue delay and, where feasible, within 72 hours of becoming aware.
purpose: Ensures regulators are promptly informed of data breaches to enable regulatory oversight and public protection.
implementationGuidance: Implement a breach detection and assessment process. Define the 72-hour notification window as a hard SLA. Prepare notification templates containing all Article 33(3) required content. Assign responsibility for notifications.
evidenceExamples: Past breach notification records with timestamps
Breach notification template with Article 33 content
bestPractices: Treat the 72-hour clock as starting from when any employee becomes aware of a possible breach
Pre-draft and legally review notification templates
Conduct breach notification exercises to test the 72-hour process

title: Communication of a Personal Data Breach to the Data Subject
description: Where the breach is likely to result in high risk to individuals, the controller shall communicate the personal data breach to the data subject without undue delay.
purpose: Ensures individuals are informed of breaches that may harm them so they can take protective measures.
implementationGuidance: Define criteria for high-risk breaches requiring data subject notification. Implement processes to notify affected individuals in clear, plain language. Maintain records of all breach communications.
evidenceExamples: Data subject notification records for high-risk breaches
Risk assessment records determining notification requirement
Notification template with required Article 34 content
bestPractices: Use clear, plain language in data subject breach notifications
Include specific actions individuals can take to protect themselves
Notify data subjects as early as possible once high risk is established

title: Data Protection Impact Assessment
description: Where processing is likely to result in a high risk to individuals (new technologies, large-scale profiling, systematic monitoring), the controller shall carry out a DPIA prior to the processing.
purpose: Ensures high-risk processing activities are assessed and risks mitigated before processing begins.
implementationGuidance: Define triggers for mandatory DPIAs. Implement a DPIA methodology. Conduct DPIAs before commencing high-risk processing. Consult the DPO. Document and follow up on DPIA recommendations.
evidenceExamples: Completed DPIA records for high-risk processing
bestPractices: Conduct DPIAs early in project design, not retrospectively
Involve DPO in all DPIA processes
Track DPIA recommendations to closure and re-assess if recommendations are not implemented

title: Prior Consultation
description: The controller shall consult the supervisory authority prior to processing where a DPIA indicates that the processing would result in a high residual risk.
purpose: Requires regulatory consultation for the highest-risk processing activities.
implementationGuidance: Define the process for prior consultation. When a DPIA results in high residual risk that cannot be mitigated, consult the relevant supervisory authority before commencing processing.
evidenceExamples: Records of prior consultation with supervisory authority
DPIA residual risk assessment showing consultation trigger
Supervisory authority response and guidance records
bestPractices: Conduct DPIAs rigorously to identify cases requiring prior consultation
Engage legal counsel before submitting for prior consultation
Document supervisory authority guidance and implement recommendations

title: Designation of the Data Protection Officer
description: A DPO must be designated where processing is carried out by a public authority, where core activities consist of large-scale systematic monitoring, or where core activities consist of large-scale processing of special categories.
purpose: Ensures organisations with high-risk processing have a dedicated expert to oversee data protection compliance.
implementationGuidance: Assess whether DPO designation is mandatory. Even if not mandatory, consider voluntary appointment. The DPO must have expert knowledge of data protection law and practice.
evidenceExamples: DPO contact published in privacy notice
bestPractices: Appoint a DPO proactively even if not strictly required for high-risk processing
Ensure DPO has sufficient resources and independence
Publish DPO contact details in privacy notice and on website

title: Position of the Data Protection Officer
description: The controller and processor shall ensure that the DPO is involved in all issues related to protection of personal data, has resources to carry out tasks, maintains expert knowledge, and does not receive instructions regarding exercise of tasks.
purpose: Ensures the DPO has the independence and resources to carry out their role effectively.
implementationGuidance: Ensure the DPO reports directly to the highest management level. Protect the DPO from conflicts of interest. Provide adequate time, budget, and support for DPO activities. Involve the DPO in all privacy-related decisions.
evidenceExamples: Records of DPO consultation on privacy matters
bestPractices: Give the DPO direct reporting access to the board
Ensure DPO is not given conflicting roles (e.g., CISO and DPO simultaneously)
Involve the DPO in DPIAs, privacy by design, and contract reviews

title: Tasks of the Data Protection Officer
description: The DPO shall inform and advise on obligations, monitor compliance, advise on DPIAs, cooperate with supervisory authorities, and act as the contact point.
purpose: Defines the specific responsibilities of the DPO role.
implementationGuidance: Document the DPO's task list and ensure they have capacity to fulfil all responsibilities. Provide access to all processing activities for monitoring. Ensure the DPO's advice is documented and considered.
evidenceExamples: DPO advice records for DPIA and high-risk processing
bestPractices: Conduct annual review of DPO task completion and resource adequacy
Document all DPO advice for accountability purposes
Provide the DPO with access to all systems and data flows for monitoring

title: General Principle for Transfers
description: Any transfer of personal data to a third country shall take place only if the conditions of Chapter V are complied with.
purpose: Establishes that cross-border data transfers require specific safeguards.
implementationGuidance: Identify all transfers of personal data to countries outside the EEA. Map each transfer to an appropriate transfer mechanism (adequacy decision, SCCs, BCRs, etc.). Do not transfer without a valid mechanism.
requiredDocuments: Legal basis for each international transfer
bestPractices: Map all international data flows as part of data mapping exercises
Prefer adequacy decisions where available
Implement SCCs for all transfers to non-adequate countries

title: Transfers on the Basis of an Adequacy Decision
description: A transfer may take place where the Commission has decided that the third country ensures an adequate level of protection.
purpose: Allows transfers to countries deemed to have equivalent data protection standards.
implementationGuidance: Identify transfers to countries covered by Commission adequacy decisions. Monitor the status of adequacy decisions as they can be invalidated (e.g., Schrems II). Maintain a fallback mechanism in case adequacy is withdrawn.
requiredDocuments: Fallback mechanism for each adequacy transfer
evidenceExamples: List of transfers by country with adequacy status
Fallback SCC or BCR for adequacy-reliant transfers
bestPractices: Monitor adequacy decision status through official European Commission sources
Maintain SCCs as a fallback for all adequacy-based transfers
Review adequacy reliance after major regulatory developments

title: Transfers Subject to Appropriate Safeguards
description: In the absence of an adequacy decision, a transfer may take place with appropriate safeguards such as Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs), codes of conduct, or certification mechanisms.
purpose: Provides mechanisms for lawful data transfers to countries without adequacy decisions.
implementationGuidance: Select the appropriate transfer mechanism for each non-adequate country. Implement SCCs for most B2B transfers. Implement BCRs for intragroup transfers if applicable. Conduct Transfer Impact Assessments (TIAs) post-Schrems II.
requiredDocuments: SCCs executed for non-adequate country transfers
evidenceExamples: Executed SCCs with non-adequate country processors/controllers
TIA records for each SCC-based transfer
bestPractices: Conduct TIAs for all SCC-based transfers to identify supplementary measures needed
Use the latest EU SCCs (2021 version)
Review and update SCCs when new versions are issued by the Commission

title: Binding Corporate Rules
description: Binding Corporate Rules approved by a competent supervisory authority allow transfers within a corporate group to countries not covered by adequacy decisions.
purpose: Provides a group-wide transfer mechanism for multinational organisations.
implementationGuidance: Assess whether BCRs are appropriate for your corporate structure. Engage lead supervisory authority for approval. Implement BCR requirements across all group entities. Monitor and update BCRs as required.
evidenceExamples: BCR implementation evidence across group entities
bestPractices: Engage a lead supervisory authority experienced in BCR approvals
BCRs require significant time and resource investment — assess proportionality
Monitor and update BCRs when group structure or processing activities change

title: Derogations for Specific Situations
description: In the absence of an adequacy decision or appropriate safeguards, transfers may occur in specific situations such as explicit consent, performance of a contract, important public interest, or defence of legal claims.
purpose: Provides narrow exceptions for international transfers in specific circumstances.
implementationGuidance: Apply Article 49 derogations only where Chapter V mechanisms are not available. Do not use derogations as a routine transfer mechanism. Document the specific grounds for each derogation-based transfer.
requiredDocuments: Documented grounds for each derogation use
bestPractices: Treat Article 49 derogations as a last resort, not a routine mechanism
Obtain legal advice before relying on derogations
Review derogation-based transfers regularly to implement proper safeguards

title: Supervisory Authority
description: Each Member State shall provide for one or more independent public authorities to be responsible for monitoring the application of GDPR.
purpose: Establishes the enforcement framework for GDPR compliance.
implementationGuidance: Identify the competent supervisory authority(ies) for your organisation. Register with the relevant authority where required. Cooperate with supervisory authority inquiries and investigations.
bestPractices: Identify your lead supervisory authority based on your main establishment in the EU
Build a positive relationship with your supervisory authority
Monitor supervisory authority guidance and decisions relevant to your sector

title: Competence
description: Each supervisory authority shall be competent for the performance of the tasks assigned and the exercise of the powers conferred on it in accordance with GDPR on the territory of its own Member State.
purpose: Clarifies which supervisory authority is competent for your organisation.
implementationGuidance: Identify which supervisory authority has competence for your main processing activities under the one-stop-shop mechanism. Understand when multiple authorities may have competence.
bestPractices: Conduct a formal one-stop-shop mechanism analysis with legal counsel
Document the basis for your lead SA identification
Monitor EDPB guidance on cross-border cases

title: Powers of Supervisory Authorities
description: Supervisory authorities have investigative, corrective, advisory, and authorisation powers including the power to impose administrative fines.
purpose: Establishes the enforcement tools available to regulators.
implementationGuidance: Understand the full range of supervisory authority powers. Implement processes to cooperate with investigations. Be prepared to respond to information requests and enforcement actions.
evidenceExamples: Legal counsel engagement for regulatory matters
bestPractices: Conduct annual GDPR audit readiness assessments
Maintain an organised evidence library for regulatory inquiries
Engage legal counsel immediately upon receiving formal supervisory authority correspondence

title: Cooperation Between the Lead Supervisory Authority and Other Supervisory Authorities
description: The lead supervisory authority shall cooperate with the other supervisory authorities concerned in cross-border cases through the one-stop-shop mechanism.
purpose: Provides a framework for consistent regulatory oversight across EU member states.
implementationGuidance: Understand how the one-stop-shop mechanism affects your organisation. Monitor decisions and guidance issued through the cooperation mechanism. Engage with your lead SA as the primary regulatory contact.
evidenceExamples: Lead SA identification and engagement records
Monitoring of cross-border EDPB decisions relevant to your sector
bestPractices: Monitor EDPB decisions from the one-stop-shop mechanism for precedents
Engage your lead SA proactively for guidance on complex matters
Track cross-border regulatory decisions that affect your industry

title: Right to Lodge a Complaint with a Supervisory Authority
description: Without prejudice to any other administrative or judicial remedy, every data subject shall have the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority if they consider that the processing of personal data infringes GDPR.
purpose: Provides individuals with a regulatory remedy for GDPR violations.
implementationGuidance: Ensure data subjects are informed of their right to complain to a supervisory authority. Implement processes to handle complaints that arrive directly. Escalate regulatory complaints appropriately.
requiredDocuments: Right to complain included in privacy notice
evidenceExamples: Privacy notice including right to complain to SA
bestPractices: Inform data subjects of their right to complain in privacy notices
Handle complaints promptly and professionally to reduce regulatory escalations
Acknowledge all complaints within 5 business days

title: Right to Compensation and Liability
description: Any person who has suffered material or non-material damage as a result of an infringement of GDPR shall have the right to receive compensation from the controller or processor.
purpose: Establishes individual rights to compensation for GDPR breaches.
implementationGuidance: Implement strong data protection controls to minimise the risk of privacy harm and resulting liability. Maintain adequate insurance coverage. Implement a compensation claims handling process.
evidenceExamples: Data protection compliance controls reducing liability exposure
bestPractices: Maintain cyber liability insurance covering GDPR compensation claims
Implement strong controls to prevent breaches that cause individual harm
Establish a formal compensation claims process with legal involvement

title: General Conditions for Imposing Administrative Fines
description: Administrative fines shall be effective, proportionate and dissuasive. They can reach up to €10M or 2% of global turnover for less serious infringements, and up to €20M or 4% of global turnover for more serious violations.
purpose: Establishes the financial penalties for GDPR non-compliance.
implementationGuidance: Understand the maximum fines applicable to your organisation based on global turnover. Use the fine levels to prioritise compliance investment. Implement controls that address the most severe infringement categories.
bestPractices: Calculate your maximum GDPR fine exposure to prioritise compliance investment
Implement controls addressing the most serious infringement categories first (Article 83(5) matters)
Document compliance programme as a mitigating factor in any enforcement action

title: Training and Awareness Programme
description: All personnel handling personal data must be trained on GDPR requirements and their specific obligations.
purpose: Ensures staff can recognise and fulfil their GDPR obligations.
implementationGuidance: Deliver mandatory GDPR training at onboarding and annually. Provide role-specific training for high-risk roles (HR, marketing, developers). Test comprehension. Track completion.
evidenceExamples: Training content covering relevant GDPR obligations by role
bestPractices: Tailor training content to role-specific GDPR risks
Require managers to confirm their team's GDPR training completion
Refresh training when GDPR guidance changes significantly

title: Privacy by Default Implementation
description: The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure that, by default, only personal data which are necessary for each specific purpose are processed.
purpose: Ensures systems collect and use the minimum personal data necessary by default.
implementationGuidance: Configure systems to collect and process minimum data by default. Require users to actively opt-in to additional data sharing. Review default settings for privacy-friendliness in all systems.
evidenceExamples: Privacy by default checklist completion records
bestPractices: Audit default settings in all systems for privacy-friendliness annually
Make privacy-friendly defaults a UAT acceptance criterion
Disable analytics and tracking by default unless users opt-in

title: Vendor Due Diligence
description: Organisations must assess and manage the privacy risks posed by vendors and sub-processors before and during engagement.
purpose: Ensures vendors and sub-processors meet GDPR requirements for personal data protection.
implementationGuidance: Conduct privacy due diligence before engaging any vendor who will process personal data. Assess their security measures, compliance posture, and subcontractor management. Update assessments periodically.
bestPractices: Make privacy due diligence part of procurement for all data-processing vendors
Request evidence of ISO 27001, SOC 2, or equivalent from critical vendors
Conduct periodic re-assessments of critical vendor privacy practices

title: Pseudonymisation and Anonymisation
description: Pseudonymisation and anonymisation are encouraged as technical measures to reduce privacy risk while enabling data use.
purpose: Enables data use for analytics and testing while reducing privacy risk through data de-identification.
implementationGuidance: Implement pseudonymisation or anonymisation for personal data used in analytics, testing, and research. Define standards for de-identification that meet regulatory expectations. Document the techniques used and their effectiveness.
bestPractices: Apply anonymisation rather than pseudonymisation where possible for analytics
Conduct re-identification risk assessments to verify anonymisation effectiveness
Use k-anonymity, l-diversity, or similar techniques for structured data anonymisation

title: Consent Management Platform
description: Organisations relying on consent should implement a consent management platform to capture, store, and manage consent records.
purpose: Provides the technical infrastructure to manage consent at scale and demonstrate compliance.
implementationGuidance: Deploy a Consent Management Platform (CMP) for web and app consent. Ensure the CMP captures granular, purpose-specific consent with timestamps. Enable easy consent withdrawal. Maintain auditable consent records.
evidenceExamples: Consent record exports showing granularity and timestamps
bestPractices: Choose a CMP compliant with IAB TCF if participating in digital advertising
Audit CMP records periodically for completeness and accuracy
Test consent withdrawal functionality to verify it works correctly

title: Cookie Compliance
description: Cookies and similar tracking technologies that process personal data require valid consent under the ePrivacy Directive and GDPR.
purpose: Ensures website and app tracking complies with privacy requirements.
implementationGuidance: Audit all cookies and tracking technologies on your website/app. Categorise them (strictly necessary, functional, analytics, marketing). Block non-essential cookies until consent is obtained. Provide a cookie preferences centre.
evidenceExamples: Cookie banner implementation with granular choices
bestPractices: Block all non-essential cookies until consent is received
Conduct cookie audits quarterly as tracking technologies change frequently
Provide a cookie preferences centre accessible at all times (not just on first visit)

title: Data Subject Request Portal
description: Organisations should implement a mechanism enabling individuals to exercise their GDPR rights efficiently.
purpose: Simplifies and systematises the fulfilment of data subject rights requests.
implementationGuidance: Implement a data subject request portal or email address. Document the identity verification procedure. Track all requests with timestamps. Report on request volumes and response times.
evidenceExamples: DSAR portal or dedicated email evidence
bestPractices: Implement a self-service DSAR portal to reduce manual effort
Aim to respond to all DSARs within 25 days to have buffer before the 30-day deadline
Report DSAR metrics (volume, type, response time) to the DPO monthly

title: Privacy Notice Maintenance
description: Privacy notices must be kept current, accurate, and accessible.
purpose: Ensures individuals always have access to accurate information about how their data is used.
implementationGuidance: Establish a privacy notice review process triggered by processing changes. Assign ownership for privacy notice maintenance. Implement version control for privacy notices. Notify data subjects of material changes.
bestPractices: Review privacy notices at least annually
Trigger immediate review when processing activities change
Notify data subjects of material changes via email rather than just updating the website

title: Privacy Programme Governance
description: A formal privacy governance structure ensures sustained GDPR compliance across the organisation.
purpose: Provides the organisational framework for managing privacy compliance on an ongoing basis.
implementationGuidance: Establish a privacy governance committee or programme. Define roles and responsibilities. Implement privacy metrics and KPIs. Report on privacy programme performance to senior management.
bestPractices: Establish quarterly privacy governance committee meetings
Measure privacy programme effectiveness through metrics (DPIAs completed, training rates, DSAR response times)
Include privacy programme status in board reporting

title: Legitimate Interests Assessment
description: Where legitimate interests is used as the legal basis, a formal Legitimate Interests Assessment (LIA) must be conducted.
purpose: Ensures legitimate interests processing is genuinely legitimate and does not override individual rights.
implementationGuidance: Conduct a three-part LIA for each legitimate interests processing activity: purpose test (is the interest legitimate?), necessity test (is processing necessary?), balancing test (do interests override data subject rights?). Document all LIAs.
requiredDocuments: Completed LIAs for each legitimate interests activity
evidenceExamples: Completed LIA documents for all legitimate interests processing
bestPractices: Conduct LIAs at the design stage of new processing activities
Review LIAs when the nature of processing changes
Document LIAs in sufficient detail to demonstrate the balancing test was genuinely conducted

title: Biometric and Health Data Controls
description: Biometric data processed for uniquely identifying individuals and health data require enhanced protection as special categories.
purpose: Ensures the most sensitive personal data categories have robust controls.
implementationGuidance: Apply enhanced access controls, encryption, and audit logging to biometric and health data. Conduct DPIAs for all biometric and health data processing. Minimise collection and retention of such data.
requiredDocuments: Biometric and health data processing register
evidenceExamples: Enhanced access controls for health/biometric systems
DPIA records for special category processing
Audit logs for special category data access
bestPractices: Apply the highest standard of security controls to special category data
Conduct DPIAs for all special category processing without exception
Minimise special category data retention to absolute minimum necessary

title: Cross-Border Data Flow Management
description: All international transfers of personal data must be identified, assessed, and covered by appropriate transfer mechanisms.
purpose: Ensures personal data is protected when transferred outside the EEA.
implementationGuidance: Map all international data flows. Identify the appropriate transfer mechanism for each. Conduct Transfer Impact Assessments (TIAs) for SCC-based transfers. Monitor the legal landscape for transfer mechanism changes.
evidenceExamples: Data flow map showing all international transfers
TIA records for each SCC-based transfer
bestPractices: Update your international transfer register whenever new vendors or processing activities are added
Conduct TIAs for transfers to high-risk countries (China, Russia, etc.)
Monitor EDPB guidance on international transfers closely

title: Incident Response and Breach Notification
description: Organisations must have a mature incident response capability capable of identifying, assessing, and notifying personal data breaches within the required timeframes.
purpose: Enables the organisation to meet its 72-hour breach notification obligation to supervisory authorities.
implementationGuidance: Integrate GDPR breach notification into the incident response plan. Define the 72-hour clock and escalation path. Prepare notification templates. Train the incident response team on GDPR notification obligations.
requiredDocuments: Breach notification templates (supervisory authority and data subjects)
evidenceExamples: Incident response plan referencing GDPR obligations
Past breach notification records within deadline
bestPractices: Treat any suspected breach as triggering the 72-hour clock pending assessment
Run annual breach notification exercises with the full incident response team
Maintain pre-approved breach notification templates to speed response

title: Children's Online Privacy Protection
description: Services likely to be accessed by children require specific measures to protect children's personal data, including age verification and parental consent.
purpose: Provides enhanced protection for the privacy of children online.
implementationGuidance: Assess whether your services are likely to be accessed by children. If so, implement age-appropriate design principles, age verification, and parental consent for users under the applicable age threshold.
bestPractices: Follow the UK ICO's Age Appropriate Design Code as a best practice standard
Default to the most privacy-protective settings for children
Conduct regular reviews of age verification effectiveness

title: Accountability Documentation
description: Controllers must maintain sufficient documentation to demonstrate GDPR compliance to supervisory authorities on request.
purpose: Supports the accountability principle by ensuring compliance can be evidenced.
implementationGuidance: Maintain a comprehensive compliance evidence library. Organise evidence by GDPR article. Conduct regular evidence reviews to ensure currency. Assign evidence ownership. Implement version control.
evidenceExamples: Evidence provided to supervisory authorities (if applicable)
bestPractices: Maintain evidence for a minimum of 3 years to cover potential investigation timelines
Conduct quarterly evidence reviews to identify gaps
Structure the evidence library to map directly to GDPR articles for efficient retrieval

title: Privacy Impact of Emerging Technologies
description: New and emerging technologies (AI, IoT, facial recognition, etc.) present novel privacy challenges requiring proactive assessment and management.
purpose: Ensures privacy risks from new technologies are identified and managed proactively.
implementationGuidance: Assess privacy implications of emerging technologies before adoption. Conduct DPIAs for AI and ML systems processing personal data. Apply privacy by design to all technology adoption projects.
bestPractices: Conduct privacy risk assessments before deploying any AI system processing personal data
Monitor EDPB guidance on AI and emerging technologies
Apply the EU AI Act requirements alongside GDPR for high-risk AI systems

title: Third-Party Data Sharing Agreements
description: All sharing of personal data with third parties must be governed by appropriate legal agreements.
purpose: Ensures third-party data sharing is legally compliant and subjects third parties to appropriate obligations.
implementationGuidance: Identify all third-party data sharing. Classify each relationship (controller-to-controller or controller-to-processor). Implement appropriate agreements (DPA for processors, data sharing agreements for joint or separate controllers).
evidenceExamples: Executed DPAs and data sharing agreements
bestPractices: Review all third-party sharing arrangements annually
Classify each sharing relationship correctly (processor vs. separate/joint controller) with legal input
Implement right-to-audit clauses in all third-party data sharing agreements

title: Retention and Deletion Management
description: Personal data must not be retained longer than necessary for its original purpose.
purpose: Implements the storage limitation principle to minimise privacy risk from excessive data retention.
implementationGuidance: Define retention periods for each personal data category aligned with legal requirements and business needs. Implement automated deletion or anonymisation processes. Monitor retention compliance through audits.
bestPractices: Automate retention enforcement wherever possible
Review retention schedules annually for legal alignment
Implement retention reminders for manually managed records

title: Accuracy of Personal Data
description: Personal data must be accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date.
purpose: Implements the accuracy principle to prevent harm from inaccurate data.
implementationGuidance: Implement data quality controls for personal data. Provide mechanisms for individuals to update their own data. Conduct periodic data accuracy audits. Address accuracy issues promptly.
bestPractices: Allow individuals to update their personal data directly through self-service portals
Conduct annual data quality audits on key personal data stores
Implement automated data quality checks at point of input

title: Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs)
description: Privacy-Enhancing Technologies reduce privacy risks while enabling data use for analytics, research, and operations.
purpose: Enables data utility while minimising privacy risks through technical innovation.
implementationGuidance: Evaluate and implement PETs appropriate to your use cases: differential privacy for statistical analysis, homomorphic encryption for computation on encrypted data, secure multi-party computation for collaborative analysis, federated learning for ML without centralised data.
evidenceExamples: Privacy risk reduction assessment from PET adoption
bestPractices: Evaluate PETs for high-risk data analytics use cases
Monitor academic and industry developments in PETs
Apply differential privacy for publishing statistical insights about personal data

title: Sensitive Data Discovery
description: Organisations must be able to locate and classify personal data across all systems to manage it in compliance with GDPR.
purpose: Ensures complete visibility of personal data for compliance management.
implementationGuidance: Deploy data discovery tools to scan all data repositories for personal and special category data. Update the RoPA based on discovery results. Remediate any unapproved or unexpected personal data stores found.
evidenceExamples: RoPA updates triggered by discovery findings
bestPractices: Run data discovery scans at least quarterly
Investigate and remediate any unexpected personal data stores found
Integrate discovery results with your data governance programme

title: Lawful Marketing Communications
description: Direct marketing communications must comply with GDPR consent requirements and the right to object to marketing.
purpose: Ensures marketing activities respect individuals' privacy rights and preferences.
implementationGuidance: Obtain valid consent for email marketing. Implement double opt-in for email lists. Honour all unsubscribe requests immediately. Suppress opted-out individuals from future campaigns. Maintain suppression lists.
bestPractices: Implement double opt-in for email marketing consent
Process unsubscribes within 10 business days
Maintain and apply suppression lists across all marketing platforms

title: GDPR Annual Programme Review
description: The GDPR compliance programme should be reviewed annually to assess effectiveness and make improvements.
purpose: Ensures the privacy programme remains effective and current as regulations and risks evolve.
implementationGuidance: Conduct an annual review of the GDPR compliance programme. Assess completion of planned activities, identify gaps, and update the programme plan. Report findings to senior management and the DPO.
requiredDocuments: Updated programme plan for following year
bestPractices: Benchmark the programme against EDPB guidance and supervisory authority enforcement actions
Involve the DPO in the annual programme review
Use the annual review to prioritise privacy investments for the coming year

title: Employee Privacy Rights Management
description: Employees have GDPR rights regarding personal data processed in the employment context including HR records, monitoring, and performance data.
purpose: Ensures employee personal data is handled lawfully and ethically.
implementationGuidance: Inform employees of their GDPR rights regarding employment data. Establish procedures for employee DSARs. Assess lawful basis for all employee monitoring. Include GDPR obligations in employment contracts.
bestPractices: Provide employees with clear privacy notices covering all HR data processing
Handle employee DSARs with the same rigour as customer DSARs
Conduct LIAs for all employee monitoring activities

title: Privacy Metrics and Reporting
description: Regular reporting on privacy metrics enables management oversight and demonstrates the programme's effectiveness.
purpose: Provides visibility into privacy programme performance for management accountability.
implementationGuidance: Define privacy KPIs (DSAR response times, training completion, breach count, DPIA completion, vendor assessment status). Report metrics to management and board quarterly. Use metrics to drive programme improvements.
bestPractices: Define 5–10 meaningful privacy KPIs that drive the right behaviours
Report privacy metrics to the board at least annually
Benchmark metrics year-over-year to demonstrate programme improvement

title: Supplier GDPR Audit Rights
description: Controllers have the right to audit processors for GDPR compliance and must include audit rights in DPAs.
purpose: Enables controllers to verify that processors are fulfilling their GDPR obligations.
implementationGuidance: Include audit rights in all DPAs. Exercise audit rights for critical processors at least annually. Review processor SOC 2 or ISO 27001 reports as an alternative to direct audits where appropriate.
requiredDocuments: Audit rights clause in DPA template
bestPractices: Conduct direct audits of highest-risk processors
Accept third-party certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001) as audit evidence for lower-risk processors
Track processor audit findings to closure

title: Profiling and Behavioural Advertising Compliance
description: Profiling of individuals and behavioural advertising require valid legal bases and transparency, with specific obligations when profiling produces significant effects.
purpose: Ensures profiling and targeted advertising activities comply with GDPR.
implementationGuidance: Map all profiling activities. Identify the legal basis for each. Provide transparency about profiling in privacy notices. Respect opt-outs. Conduct DPIAs for high-risk profiling. Implement controls to prevent discriminatory profiling.
evidenceExamples: Profiling activity register with legal basis
bestPractices: Conduct DPIAs for all significant profiling activities
Implement easy opt-out from profiling separate from other processing
Monitor for discriminatory profiling patterns

title: Privacy Programme Staffing and Resources
description: The privacy programme must be adequately resourced with qualified staff to fulfil GDPR obligations.
purpose: Ensures the organisation has sufficient capacity to manage privacy compliance effectively.
implementationGuidance: Assess privacy staffing needs based on the scale and complexity of data processing. Ensure the privacy team has relevant qualifications and ongoing training. Provide adequate budget for privacy tools and external support.
evidenceExamples: Privacy team org chart with qualifications
Privacy staff training and certification records
bestPractices: Staff the privacy function proportionately to data processing complexity
Invest in privacy management platforms to extend team capacity
Maintain an external privacy law firm relationship for complex matters

title: Regulatory Horizon Scanning
description: Organisations must monitor regulatory developments to maintain GDPR compliance as the regulatory landscape evolves.
purpose: Ensures the privacy programme adapts to new regulatory requirements and guidance.
implementationGuidance: Subscribe to EDPB guidance updates, supervisory authority newsletters, and privacy law publications. Monitor enforcement decisions for lessons learned. Brief management on material regulatory developments.
requiredDocuments: Compliance impact assessment for regulatory changes
evidenceExamples: Programme updates triggered by regulatory changes
bestPractices: Assign responsibility for regulatory horizon scanning to DPO or legal
Conduct quarterly regulatory update briefings for the privacy committee
Assess compliance impact of all material regulatory changes within 30 days

title: Privacy in Mergers, Acquisitions and Restructuring
description: M&A activities and corporate restructuring raise significant GDPR considerations including due diligence on target privacy compliance and notification obligations.
purpose: Ensures privacy risks from corporate transactions are identified and managed.
implementationGuidance: Include GDPR compliance assessment in M&A due diligence. Assess whether the transaction triggers notification obligations to supervisory authorities or data subjects. Implement integration plans addressing privacy compliance.
bestPractices: Involve privacy legal counsel in M&A due diligence teams
Assess target companies' GDPR compliance as a valuation factor
Plan for privacy integration in the first 100 days post-merger

title: Dark Patterns Prevention
description: Organisations must not use deceptive design patterns (dark patterns) that undermine users' ability to make genuine privacy choices.
purpose: Ensures consent and other privacy choices are genuinely free and informed.
implementationGuidance: Audit user interfaces for dark patterns (misleading pre-ticked boxes, difficult-to-find opt-out, visual pressure to accept). Remove all dark patterns. Design consent flows to be genuinely neutral and easy to understand.
evidenceExamples: A/B testing showing neutral design of consent options
bestPractices: Conduct external UX review of consent flows from a privacy perspective
Apply EDPB Dark Patterns guidance as a design standard
Test consent flows with real users to verify comprehension and ease

title: Legal Basis Documentation
description: All personal data processing activities must have a documented lawful basis maintained and reviewed.
purpose: Provides the foundational legal justification for all personal data processing.
implementationGuidance: Document the lawful basis for every processing activity in the RoPA. Review bases when processing changes. Reassess consent when the processing purpose changes. Archive historical legal basis documentation.
requiredDocuments: Legal basis documentation by processing activity
RoPA with legal basis column completed
evidenceExamples: Completed RoPA showing legal basis for each activity
Legal counsel review of legal basis selections
bestPractices: Have legal counsel review legal basis selections for high-risk processing
Review legal bases annually as part of RoPA maintenance
Document the analysis behind each legal basis selection

title: Privacy Budget and Investment Planning
description: Adequate privacy investment is essential for maintaining GDPR compliance and reducing regulatory risk.
purpose: Ensures sufficient financial resources are allocated to sustain privacy compliance.
implementationGuidance: Define an annual privacy budget covering tools, staff, training, legal advice, and external assessments. Align investment with risk — higher-risk processing requires greater investment. Report budget allocation to senior management.
evidenceExamples: Investment in privacy tools and resources evidence
bestPractices: Benchmark privacy investment against industry peers
Align privacy budget with risk and regulatory exposure
Present privacy ROI analysis to leadership to secure adequate funding

title: Customer-Facing Privacy Communications
description: Customer-facing privacy communications must be clear, accessible, and accurate across all touchpoints.
purpose: Builds customer trust through transparent privacy communications.
implementationGuidance: Review all customer-facing communications (website, app, email, support) for privacy accuracy and clarity. Ensure privacy notices are easy to find and understand. Train customer support on handling privacy inquiries.
bestPractices: Test privacy notices with real customers for comprehension
Make the privacy notice accessible within 1-2 clicks from any page
Train all customer-facing staff on GDPR rights and how to direct privacy inquiries

title: Employee Monitoring Compliance
description: Employee monitoring (email scanning, device monitoring, CCTV, attendance tracking) must comply with GDPR and employment law.
purpose: Ensures employee monitoring is lawful, proportionate, and transparent.
implementationGuidance: Conduct a LIA or other legal basis assessment for all employee monitoring. Inform employees transparently about all monitoring. Minimise monitoring to what is strictly necessary. Document all monitoring activities.
requiredDocuments: Monitoring LIA or legal basis assessment
bestPractices: Apply proportionality test: is monitoring the least intrusive means of achieving the objective?
Inform employees of all monitoring in employment contracts and privacy notices
Conduct DPIAs for highly intrusive monitoring (keystroke logging, continuous screen capture)

title: Healthcare and Clinical Data Privacy
description: Health data is a special category requiring explicit consent or Article 9(2) exemption, plus enhanced security and governance.
purpose: Provides the highest level of privacy protection for sensitive health information.
implementationGuidance: Map all health data processing. Justify under Article 9(2). Apply enhanced access controls, encryption, and audit logging. Conduct DPIAs. Implement strict retention controls. Ensure clinical staff GDPR training.
requiredDocuments: Enhanced security controls for health data
evidenceExamples: Enhanced access controls for clinical systems
bestPractices: Apply pseudonymisation to health data for research purposes
Segregate health data systems from general IT environment
Conduct annual security audits of systems containing health data

title: Data Governance Framework
description: A data governance framework provides the policies, processes, and standards for managing personal data as a corporate asset.
purpose: Ensures personal data is managed in a structured, compliant, and accountable way.
implementationGuidance: Establish a data governance committee. Define data stewardship roles. Implement data classification, lifecycle management, and quality standards. Integrate data governance with privacy compliance.
bestPractices: Align data governance and privacy governance into a unified programme
Assign data stewards for all major personal data categories
Conduct quarterly data governance committee meetings

title: Third-Country Data Transfer Impact Assessment
description: Post-Schrems II, organisations must conduct Transfer Impact Assessments (TIAs) for data transfers based on SCCs to assess whether destination country laws undermine the protection offered.
purpose: Ensures personal data transferred under SCCs receives equivalent protection as within the EEA.
implementationGuidance: Conduct TIAs for all SCC-based transfers. Assess destination country laws, particularly surveillance and intelligence laws. Implement supplementary measures where necessary. Document all TIA outcomes.
evidenceExamples: TIA records for each non-adequate country transfer
Legal analysis of destination country laws
bestPractices: Use the EDPB's TIA recommendations as the methodology framework
Engage local legal counsel in destination countries for law assessment
Implement encryption in transit as a universal supplementary measure

title: Website Analytics Privacy
description: Website analytics tools that process personal data (IP addresses, behaviour tracking) must comply with GDPR requirements.
purpose: Ensures web analytics respects visitor privacy rights.
implementationGuidance: Assess whether your analytics tools process personal data. Obtain valid consent for analytics cookies. Consider privacy-friendly analytics alternatives. Configure analytics to anonymise IPs and limit data retention.
bestPractices: Configure Google Analytics (or equivalent) to anonymise IP addresses
Block analytics until consent is received via CMP
Consider privacy-first analytics tools (Matomo, Plausible) as GDPR-friendly alternatives

title: Privacy in Human Resources
description: HR processes (recruitment, performance management, payroll, benefits) involve significant personal data processing requiring GDPR compliance.
purpose: Ensures employee and candidate personal data is managed lawfully and transparently.
implementationGuidance: Map all HR personal data processing. Identify lawful basis for each. Provide employee and candidate privacy notices. Implement data minimisation in HR systems. Define retention periods for HR records.
evidenceExamples: Candidate privacy notice in recruitment process
bestPractices: Conduct a comprehensive HR data mapping exercise
Review HR data retention schedules against employment law requirements in each jurisdiction
Ensure recruitment platforms' DPAs are in place before using them

title: Consent Withdrawal Process
description: Individuals must be able to withdraw consent as easily as they gave it, and withdrawal must be honoured promptly.
purpose: Ensures consent withdrawal rights are genuine and effective.
implementationGuidance: Implement consent withdrawal mechanisms equally accessible as consent grant. Process withdrawals without delay. Cease processing based on the withdrawn consent immediately. Notify processors of withdrawal.
evidenceExamples: Processor notification records for consent withdrawals
bestPractices: Implement one-click withdrawal for online consent
Process withdrawal immediately (or within a maximum 24 hours for technical reasons)
Test the withdrawal journey to verify it is as simple as the consent journey

title: Privacy Risk Management Integration
description: Privacy risks should be integrated into the organisation's enterprise risk management framework.
purpose: Ensures privacy risks receive appropriate attention at the enterprise risk management level.
implementationGuidance: Include privacy risks in the enterprise risk register. Define privacy risk appetite. Escalate material privacy risks to the executive level. Report on privacy risk status in board risk reporting.
requiredDocuments: Privacy risks in enterprise risk register
evidenceExamples: Enterprise risk register with privacy entries
bestPractices: Align privacy risk ratings with the enterprise risk management methodology
Escalate high-rated privacy risks to the Risk Committee
Review privacy risk ratings quarterly against emerging threats and regulatory developments

title: Digital Rights Management
description: Digital rights management (DRM) and access controls for digital content must respect GDPR principles for personal data involved.
purpose: Ensures DRM systems handle personal data lawfully.
implementationGuidance: Assess whether DRM systems process personal data. Identify lawful basis. Provide transparency. Apply data minimisation. Define retention periods for DRM-related personal data.
evidenceExamples: Privacy notice covering DRM data processing
bestPractices: Apply GDPR principles during DRM system design
Minimise personal data collected for DRM purposes
Provide clear transparency about DRM data processing in product privacy notices

title: Privacy Self-Assessment and Audit
description: Regular privacy self-assessments and audits identify compliance gaps and drive programme improvement.
purpose: Maintains an accurate understanding of the organisation's privacy compliance status.
implementationGuidance: Conduct annual privacy self-assessment against all applicable GDPR requirements. Engage third-party auditors for independent privacy audits. Track and remediate findings from assessments and audits.
bestPractices: Use NIST Privacy Framework or ISO 27701 as assessment frameworks
Engage external privacy auditors every 2–3 years
Provide privacy assessment results to senior management and the DPO

title: Special Category Data in Recruitment
description: Recruitment processes may incidentally collect special category data (disability information, health status) requiring careful GDPR management.
purpose: Ensures sensitive data collected during recruitment is handled lawfully and not used for discrimination.
implementationGuidance: Avoid collecting special category data during recruitment unless strictly necessary (e.g., reasonable adjustments). Where collected, identify Article 9(2) grounds. Apply enhanced protections. Limit access to necessary personnel only.
requiredDocuments: Article 9(2) grounds for recruitment special category data
Recruitment system access controls for special category data
evidenceExamples: Recruitment privacy notice covering special category data
Access controls for disability/health information in HR systems
Training records for recruitment staff on special category data
bestPractices: Separate optional disclosure of disability information from the main recruitment flow
Train hiring managers to handle disability information appropriately
Store disability/adjustment information separately from general recruitment records

title: Privacy Community of Practice
description: A privacy community of practice brings together privacy champions across the business to embed privacy culture organisation-wide.
purpose: Scales privacy awareness and compliance capacity beyond the central privacy team.
implementationGuidance: Establish a network of privacy champions in each business unit. Provide regular briefings and training. Empower champions to identify and escalate privacy issues. Recognise and reward privacy champion contributions.
evidenceExamples: Issues escalated by champions and their resolution
bestPractices: Recruit privacy champions who are respected in their business units
Provide monthly briefings to champions on current privacy issues
Give champions a direct channel to the DPO for urgent matters

title: Processing Activity Changes Notification
description: Material changes to processing activities must be assessed for GDPR compliance and may require updating privacy notices and conducting DPIAs.
purpose: Ensures GDPR compliance is maintained as business processing activities evolve.
implementationGuidance: Implement a change notification process requiring GDPR impact assessment for significant processing changes. Trigger DPIA when required. Update RoPA and privacy notices. Notify data subjects of material changes where required.
requiredDocuments: GDPR impact assessment for processing changes
evidenceExamples: Privacy notice version history showing updates for processing changes
bestPractices: Integrate GDPR impact assessment into change management processes
Set a low threshold for triggering GDPR review of changes
Update privacy notices proactively before implementing changes that affect data subjects

title: Privacy in Software Development Lifecycle
description: Privacy must be integrated into the software development lifecycle to ensure systems are privacy-compliant from inception.
purpose: Implements Privacy by Design for all software development activities.
implementationGuidance: Include privacy requirements in system specifications. Conduct privacy reviews at design and testing stages. Include privacy testing in acceptance criteria. Train developers on privacy-sensitive coding practices.
bestPractices: Make privacy review a mandatory gate in the development lifecycle
Train developers to recognise and handle personal data appropriately in code
Include privacy regression testing as part of the CI/CD pipeline

title: IoT and Connected Devices Privacy
description: Internet of Things (IoT) devices that collect or process personal data require specific privacy controls and transparency.
purpose: Addresses the unique privacy challenges posed by pervasive IoT data collection.
implementationGuidance: Inventory all IoT devices processing personal data. Conduct DPIAs. Implement technical controls for IoT data minimisation, consent, and security. Provide clear disclosure about IoT data collection.
bestPractices: Apply data minimisation at IoT device design stage
Provide clear, persistent privacy notices for IoT devices
Implement on-device processing where possible to minimise data transmission

title: AI and Machine Learning Privacy
description: AI and ML systems processing personal data require careful GDPR compliance including transparency, explainability, and DPIA requirements.
purpose: Addresses the unique privacy challenges of AI systems including opacity and profiling risks.
implementationGuidance: Conduct DPIAs for all AI systems processing personal data. Implement explainability for automated decisions. Monitor AI systems for discriminatory outcomes. Provide transparency about AI-driven processing.
bestPractices: Apply algorithmic fairness testing to AI systems processing personal data
Implement human review for significant AI-driven decisions
Monitor AI systems for privacy-violating outputs or bias

title: Emergency and Crisis Privacy
description: Emergency situations may create pressure to deviate from normal GDPR requirements, requiring clear guidance on the permissible exceptions.
purpose: Ensures privacy obligations are met even in emergency and crisis situations.
implementationGuidance: Define clear guidance on GDPR exceptions applicable in emergencies (vital interests, public health). Document any emergency derogations used with justification. Return to normal compliance as soon as the emergency passes.
evidenceExamples: Vital interests processing records with justification
Return to normal compliance plan following emergency
bestPractices: Pre-draft emergency privacy guidance covering vital interests and public health scenarios
Document all emergency processing with precise justification and time bounds
Review emergency measures regularly to ensure they remain necessary and proportionate

title: Privacy Certifications and Seals
description: GDPR-approved certification mechanisms can demonstrate compliance and build customer trust.
purpose: Provides a market-recognised mechanism for demonstrating GDPR compliance.
implementationGuidance: Assess the availability of GDPR-approved certifications in your sector. Consider pursuing certification as a competitive differentiator. Implement the certification requirements. Maintain certification through regular review.
bestPractices: Monitor EDPB's list of approved certification criteria for your sector
Pursue ISO 27701 as an internationally recognised privacy management certification
Communicate certification status clearly to customers and prospects

title: Privacy Culture Programme
description: Building a culture of privacy across the organisation is essential for sustained GDPR compliance.
purpose: Creates an environment where privacy is valued and respected by all employees.
implementationGuidance: Launch privacy culture initiatives including awareness campaigns, privacy champions, leadership role modelling, and recognition for privacy-positive behaviours. Measure culture through surveys and tracking of voluntary privacy reports.
evidenceExamples: Culture survey results showing privacy awareness levels
bestPractices: Run annual privacy awareness campaigns for Data Privacy Day (January 28)
Celebrate privacy successes publicly within the organisation
Measure privacy culture through annual employee surveys
